title: Farmhouse Hall Tree
summary: A tall entryway piece combining a storage cubby bench below with a jigsaw-shaped hook rail above — a genuine bench-plus-hooks hall tree distinct from this catalog's simpler entryway benches.
description:
This hall tree is a genuinely two-part build: a bench-height storage cubby section on plywood, topped by a tall jigsaw-shaped hook panel that rises well above seat height — combining what this catalog's other entryway benches keep as separate pieces (a bench and a wall-mounted hook rail) into one continuous unit. Build the bench storage area first from ripped plywood, following the cubby layout, then add bottom supports that transfer a sitter's weight straight down to the floor rather than through the cubby dividers alone.

**The face frame comes next, built with pocket holes and attached with finish nails and glue** — this is what gives the piece its finished furniture look rather than exposed plywood edges. Base moulding wraps the bottom of the bench section, and the plan notes a genuine option here: raise the bottom 1" and use a wider 3-1/4" moulding instead of the standard 2-1/4" if you want a chunkier base profile.

The hook panel's shape is cut from a 1x12 with a jigsaw — **clamp the two panel pieces together and sand until they match exactly**, the same matching technique this catalog uses for other paired jigsaw cuts, since a hand-shaped profile that doesn't mirror cleanly is immediately visible on a piece this prominent. The back panel goes on last, shown split into three separate boards specifically so the whole assembly can still fit through a car trunk or door if you're transporting it after building.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

cutList.part: Hook panel back frame mid rails

cutList.note: Shaped with a jigsaw; clamp together and sand to match.
Split into three boards for transport; combined width covers 48".
Cut to fit around the bench base.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Break the sheets down first. From the 3/4" plywood strips, all 15-3/4" wide: one bench top 49-1/2", one bench bottom 46-1/2", two cubby dividers 14", and two bench end panels 17-1/4" — three of the strips cover all six pieces. From the 1x3: two bottom support rails 46-1/2", one face frame mid rail 45", two hook panel back frame rails 47", six hook panel back frame mid rails 12". From the 1x2: one face frame top rail 49-1/2", one face frame front rail 48", two face frame stiles 15-3/4". From the 1x4: two hook panel back frame sides 56" and one hook rail top trim 41". From the 1x6: the 41" top cap. From the 1x12: two 24" blanks for the shaped sides. From the 1/2" sheet: three back sections 16" x 74", cut along the length of the sheet so all three come off one panel. Three lumber checks before you buy: the 1x3 parts total 304" and three 8 ft boards give only 288", so get a fourth; a 56" frame side and the 41" trim will not share a board (97" on a 96" board), so the two 1x4s listed will not do and you need three; and the two 24" shaped blanks come to exactly 48", the whole 1x12, with no allowance for the kerf.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build the bench storage area
steps.body: The two 17-1/4" end panels run the full height of the bench box and the 46-1/2" bottom sits between them, which makes the box 48" wide overall — the same 48" as the face frame front rail and as the three 16" back sections together, so those two parts are your check that you have it the right way round. Stand the two 14" cubby dividers on the bottom to split the space, and join everything with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, holes placed where the face frame or the back will later hide them. Fit the 49-1/2" top last, overhanging 3/4" at each end. Measure both diagonals across the front and adjust until they match before the last screws go in.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the bottom supports
steps.body: The two 1x3 rails are 46-1/2" — the same length as the bench bottom — so they run the length of the box, not across it. Fit one under the front edge of the bottom and one under the back, fastening with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws into the bottom and into the end panels. This is what puts a sitter's weight on a rail that reaches the floor instead of hanging the whole load off the cubby dividers and the pocket screws holding them.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the frame flat on the bench from the 49-1/2" top rail, the 48" front rail, the two 15-3/4" stiles and the 45" mid rail, joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Check it square by its diagonals while it is still flat and unattached — a frame assembled flat is easy to keep square, and one built piece by piece against the box is not. Then glue it on and set 1-1/4" finish nails through the face, punching the heads below the surface so they fill cleanly. The frame is what covers the plywood edges, and it is the single thing that makes this read as furniture rather than a plywood box.

steps.title: Add the base moulding
steps.body: Cut the 2-1/4" base moulding to wrap the bottom of the bench: 48" across the front and a 15-3/4" return along each end, with the two front corners mitered at 45°. That is 79-1/2" of the 96" length, so there is stock spare for a mis-cut. Cut the front piece first with both miters, fit it, then cut each return with one miter and one square end at the back. Nail it on with 1-1/4" finish nails into the end panels and the bottom support rails. If you want a chunkier base, the plan's own alternative is to raise the bench bottom 1" and run 3-1/4" moulding instead of the 2-1/4".

steps.title: Build the hook panel back frame
steps.body: The two 1x4 sides are 56" and they stand on top of the finished bench section — 17-1/4" of end panel plus the 3/4" top is 18", and 18" plus 56" is 74", exactly the length of the back panel sections, so the back is your check on the overall height. Join the 47" rails and the six 12" mid rails to the sides with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, working on a flat surface and checking the diagonals. One thing to sort out before you screw anything home: the 47" rails and the 41" top trim and cap imply two different frame widths on a 48" bench, and the plan's own numbers do not settle which is right — dry-fit the frame on the bench, mark the rails against the width you actually have, and trim them to that.

steps.title: Add the top moulding
steps.body: Attach the 41" 1x4 top trim across the face of the hook panel frame at the top, glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, then set the 41" 1x6 cap flat on top of it and nail down through the cap into the trim and the frame sides. Keep the cap's overhang even at the front and at both ends — this is at eye level on a piece nearly 6 ft tall, and an uneven reveal there is the first thing anyone notices.

steps.title: Shape and attach the hook panel sides
steps.body: Mark the shaped profile on one 24" 1x12 blank and cut it with the jigsaw, staying a little outside the line rather than trying to cut to it. Then clamp the two blanks together face to face and sand the pair down to the line as one piece — that is what guarantees the two sides mirror each other exactly, and on a piece this prominent a hand-shaped profile that does not mirror is immediately visible. Keep the sander square to the edge so the profile stays perpendicular through the full 3/4" thickness. Glue and fasten them in place with 1-1/4" finish nails.

steps.title: Attach the back and finish
steps.body: Nail the three 16" x 74" back sections on with 1-1/4" finish nails every 6" into the frame members behind them, keeping the outer edges flush with the sides of the unit. Three separate boards rather than one 48" panel is deliberate — it is what lets the finished piece still go through a doorway or into a car. Mark all five coat hooks before drilling any of them, spacing them evenly across the hook rail, and drive their screws into the 1x3 frame members rather than into the 1/2" back alone, which will not hold a loaded coat. Sand every surface, fill the nail holes, and prime and paint or stain and seal.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Farmhouse hall tree with a storage bench and coat hook panel
